div.p: To all who shall see or hear these letters, Master William of Lindsay, dean of Glasgow, wishes eternal salvation in the Lord. Let all people know that Abbot [Herbert] and the monastic community of Kelso have in our presence resigned and quitclaimed for ever any right they used to have in the lands and pastures of Mow within the portion belonging to the lord Walter son of Alan, Steward of Scotland, and also the right of common that they used to have in the moor of Innerwick to the same Walter and his heirs and their attorneys; no right in the same is to be reclaimed in return for a portion of the said moor of Innerwick from the same William in the exchange bestowed by these boundaries, namely, from Birk Cleugh Bothwell going up by the small stream which falls from Scullins as far as Scullins Head where the ditch is drawn, and by that ditch as far as the road which stretches from Rise Lea towards Innerwick, and so from that road going up by the ditch eastwards as far as a certain furrow, and so from the furrow going down by the ditch as far as ‘Meldrescloesheued’, and so by the little stream of ‘Meldrescloesheued’ continually going down by Monynut, as far as the edge of Easter Wide Hope, and so going up by Easter Wide Hope westwards next to the bounds of ‘Ellum’ and of Rise Lea, as far as Wester Wide Hope, and from Wester Wide Hope as far as Whiteadder, and from Whiteadder far as Birk Cleugh, and so continually going down by Birk Cleugh as far as the said little stream which falls from Scullins. With these witnesses: the lord William, by the grace of God, bishop of Glasgow, Chancellor of Scotland; the lord William, abbot of Paisley; Master Hugh, archdeacon of Glasgow; and many others, among whom there were Master William of Greenlaw, Master Abel, and Master Peter of Alinton, who for the greater security of thie matter together with us have applied their own seal to the present written deed. Done at Kelso in the year of grace . . .
